summary: Starbucks buys 100% arabica coffee from more than 450,000 farmers across roughly 30 countries, grouped into three growing regions it calls the 'Coffee Belt.' Starbucks does not publish an exact percentage-of-purchases breakdown by country, so the chart below reflects the number of sourcing countries and representative examples per region rather than purchase volume.

regions.share: Largest share of purchases — Starbucks describes Latin America as providing its most consistent year-round quality and the base of most of its blends
regions.flavorProfile: Bright acidity with spice and nutty/chocolate undertones

regions.share: Smaller but prized volume — sourced for distinctive, complex single-origin lots
regions.flavorProfile: Fruity, floral and winey with bright citrus and berry acidity

regions.share: Smallest share by country count — used for earthy, full-bodied blends like Sumatra and Starbucks' own Yunnan, China sourcing
regions.flavorProfile: Earthy, herbal and full-bodied with low acidity

cafePractices.name: C.A.F.E. Practices (Coffee and Farmer Equity)
cafePractices.verifiedShare: 99% of coffee ethically sourced and third-party verified
cafePractices.indicatorCount: 200+ indicators across economic, social and environmental criteria
cafePractices.update2025: A two-year revision launched a new Tier 1 core-standard in 2025, with progressive Tiers 2 and 3 to follow, rewarding best-in-class farms.

farmerNetwork.researchFarm: Hacienda Alsacia, Costa Rica — Starbucks' own experimental coffee farm, used to test rust-resistant, climate-resilient varietals shared with the wider farmer network.
